target.title: fix(cron): keep a due recurring run when an edit re-saves the same schedule
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Editing a recurring cron job (`every` or `cron`) while one of its slots is already due silently drops that pending run. The control UI and `cron edit` resubmit the job's schedule on save, and any update that carries `schedule` (or `enabled`) advances `state.nextRunAtMs` to the next future slot without executing the slot that was already due. For an hourly job edited a few seconds after its slot came due, that hour's run never fires; for a daily job, that day's run is lost. For `every` jobs the same edit also re-phases the cadence anchor to "now", shifting every future fire time.

Root cause: `add()` and `remove()` were fixed (#94323) to recompute through `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance`, which preserves a past-due `nextRunAtMs` so the pending run still fires. `update()` was never converted; it advances unconditionally whenever the patch carries a schedule or enabled field, even when the schedule is unchanged.

`src/cron/service/ops.ts` (before):

```ts
const scheduleChanged = patch.schedule !== undefined;
const enabledChanged = patch.enabled !== undefined;
...
if (scheduleChanged || enabledChanged) {
  if (isJobEnabled(nextJob)) {
    nextJob.state.nextRunAtMs = computeJobNextRunAtMs(nextJob, now);
  }
  ...
}
```

`computeJobNextRunAtMs` returns the strictly-future next slot, so a still-due `nextRunAtMs <= now` is overwritten. The `every` anchor backfill compounds it: when a re-save omits the internal `anchorMs`, the backfill mints `anchorMs = now`, re-phasing the cadence.

## Why This Change Was Made

Only advance the next run when the scheduling inputs actually changed, using the existing `cronSchedulingInputsEqual` helper (the same helper `service/store.ts` already uses to decide whether stored timer state is still valid). Inherit the previous cadence anchor when an `every` re-save omits it, so an idempotent edit keeps its existing phase.

`src/cron/service/ops.ts` (after):

```ts
const schedulingInputsChanged =
  (patch.schedule !== undefined || patch.enabled !== undefined) &&
  !cronSchedulingInputsEqual(job, nextJob);
...
if (schedulingInputsChanged) {
  if (isJobEnabled(nextJob)) {
    nextJob.state.nextRunAtMs = computeJobNextRunAtMs(nextJob, now);
  }
  ...
}
```

A genuine schedule change (different `everyMs`, `expr`, `tz`, or an enable transition) still recomputes exactly as before; only the unchanged re-save now preserves a due slot. This is the right boundary: the defect is in the cron service `update()` op that owns next-run state for CRUD edits, the sibling load-time path (`service/store.ts invalidateStaleNextRunOnScheduleChange`) already gates on the same identity, and `add()`/`remove()` already preserve due slots via maintenance recompute. The fix brings `update()` to parity and preserves #15905 (genuine edits still recompute).

## User Impact

Recurring cron jobs no longer skip a fire when the user edits them during an already-due slot, and `every` jobs keep their cadence phase across edits instead of drifting to the edit time. Without this, any save that resubmits the schedule (the normal control-UI behavior) can silently swallow a scheduled run.

## Evidence

Drove the real `CronService.update()` against a real on-disk cron store with an injected clock, on pristine main and on the patched tree with identical inputs. The only stub is the isolated-agent job runner (the external execution boundary); the store, schedule math, anchor backfill, and update op are all real. Steps: add an `every: 10s` job, fire it once, advance the clock 50ms past the next due slot, then call `update(jobId, { schedule: { kind: "every", everyMs: 10000 } })` and read back `state.nextRunAtMs` and `schedule.anchorMs`.

```
# BEFORE (pristine main)
due slot (pending, unfired): nextRunAtMs=1765585820005 (base+1820005)
clock at edit time:          now=1765585820055 (base+1820055)
after idempotent re-save:    nextRunAtMs=1765585830055 (base+1830055), anchorMs=base+1820055
RESULT: due slot dropped, advanced 10050ms, this run is skipped
# AFTER (this patch, identical inputs)
due slot (pending, unfired): nextRunAtMs=1765585820005 (base+1820005)
clock at edit time:          now=1765585820055 (base+1820055)
after idempotent re-save:    nextRunAtMs=1765585820005 (base+1820005), anchorMs=base+0
RESULT: due slot preserved, pending run still fires
```

The due slot's `nextRunAtMs` is retained (1765585820005) instead of jumping a full interval ahead, and the cadence anchor stays at its original phase instead of re-phasing to the edit time, so the pending run still fires.

Additional checks: a colocated regression test (`every` and `cron` cases) fails on pristine main and passes with the patch; the add/remove/ops/apply-patch/at-reschedule suites stay green (178 cases); oxlint and oxfmt are clean on the changed files. Not covered: no live gateway or real isolated-agent execution; full project typecheck not run on this box (the one new call mirrors the existing un-cast `cronSchedulingInputsEqual(CronJob, CronJob)` call in `service/store.ts`).
target.author: yetval
